generations.text:
**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with a trembling hand, the wind howling like a wounded beast at my door. The sea has been restless all day, and the sky is a bruised shade of purple, heavy with the promise of something terrible. I have watched the waves rise and fall like a heartbeat, and I have thought of you more than I have ever thought of anything in my life.

Five years, Eleanor. Five long, lonely years since you left this place. I have not spoken to you, not even in passing, and I know you have not spoken to me. We have both been silent, like two ships that once sailed in the same direction but parted ways in a storm.

I have always believed that a lighthouse keeper must be strong. That he must be steady in the face of the tempest. But tonight, I am not so sure. The storm is coming, and I fear I may not be able to hold the light for you. I fear I may not be able to hold myself together.

I have not told you this before, but I have always loved you more than I have ever loved anything else in this world. I have watched over you from afar, hoping that one day you would return. I have lit the lamp every night, not just for the sailors, but for you. I have whispered your name to the wind, hoping it would carry it to you, even if only in the silence of the night.

I

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may find you in the same bitter cold that grips this lighthouse tonight. It is now the eve of the storm, and the wind howls like a wounded beast, rattling the timbers of this old tower as though it seeks to tear it down. I write with trembling hands and a heart heavy with the weight of years unspoken.

Five years have passed since you left, and yet the echoes of your footsteps still haunt these halls. I have not seen you, nor have I heard your voice, and I confess, it has been a long, lonely vigil. The sea is a cruel mistress, and the loneliness of this post has grown with each passing tide. But tonight, as the storm brews on the horizon, I find myself wondering if I will survive it—or if I will be swallowed by the very thing I have sworn to guard.

You were always so full of fire, Eleanor. I remember the way you would light your candle and stand by the window, watching the waves as though they were a story waiting to be told. You had a way of making the sea seem less daunting, less cruel. I used to tell you that the light was a promise, a beacon for those lost at sea, but I never thought it would be a promise I would have to make to myself.

I have not been the same since you left. The light has dimmed, not

**The Lighthouse Keeper’s Letter**

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a beast at the door, the sea roaring as if it would swallow the very stones of this cliff. The lantern flickers, and I fear the light may go out before dawn, as it has done many a time in the past. But tonight is different. Tonight, I feel the weight of the sea pressing down upon me, and I wonder if it is the storm, or the burden of years unspoken, that threatens to break me.

Five years have passed since you left. Five years since I last saw your face, heard your voice, or felt the warmth of your presence. I have kept this lighthouse, day after day, year after year, as though it were a promise to you, to the daughter I once loved and lost. I have lit the beacon for every ship that passed, every soul that might have been lost to the dark, and yet I have not lit the lamp of my own heart.

I have not spoken to you in years, Eleanor. Not because I have forgotten you, but because I have not known how to say the words that have been lodged in my throat since the day you turned away. I have tried to write, to send letters, to call out to you from the cliffs, but the sea has taken them all, or the wind has scattered them like leaves upon the shore.

You were a

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may not find you at all. I have penned these words with trembling hands, for the wind howls like a banshee through the lighthouse, and the sea roars as if it would swallow the very earth. Tonight, the storm is unlike any I have known, and I find myself more alone than I ever have been—both in the world and in my heart.

Five years have passed since the last time we spoke, and in that time, I have come to understand the weight of silence. You were never the one to reach out, and I, in my pride, allowed the distance to grow. I told myself that I was waiting for the right moment, the right words, the right time. But time, my dear Eleanor, is not a thing to be waited for. It is a thing to be lived, and I have lived in regret and longing, wondering if you still remember the man I once was.

You were always my light, my guiding star. I used to tell the sailors that the lighthouse was not just a beacon for the ships, but a symbol of hope, of love, of the unyielding spirit to endure. I thought that same light would guide you back to me, that the glow of our shared memories would be enough to pull you through the darkness. But I was wrong. You turned away, and I did not follow.

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a wounded beast outside these walls. The sea, that old adversary, roars with a fury I’ve never known. The lighthouse, my life’s work, stands defiant against the storm, but I fear it may not stand much longer. And if it falls, so too may I.

Five years have passed since I last saw you, and though the years have worn me down, the silence between us has worn me to the bone. You once called me “Dad,” a word that once brought warmth to my heart. Now it feels like a dagger, sharp and cold, lodged in my chest. I’ve tried to reach out, to mend what time and distance have broken, but you’ve turned your back, and I’ve not dared to follow.

I know you’ve been through much. I’ve heard the whispers of your life in the city, of the life you chose, the life I could not give you. I’ve heard the stories of the man you once loved, the man who left you behind. I’ve heard the pain, and I’ve felt it in my own heart. But I’ve also heard the truth—of your anger, of your disappointment, of the way you’ve closed yourself off from the world, and from me.

Tonight, the storm is coming. The sky is black as pitch, the waves crashing like the thunder in my soul. I’ve

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with a hand that trembles not only from the cold but from the weight of the words I must speak. The wind howls like a wounded beast outside this tower, and the sea roars as though it would swallow the very earth. I have not spoken to you in five years, and yet, as this storm gathers, I find myself compelled to reach out—to you, my daughter, my only child.

I know you do not answer letters. I know you have turned your back on me, on this life, on this tower, on the sea that has been both my refuge and my curse. I know you have heard the stories—of the lighthouse keeper who never leaves his post, who is consumed by the sea, who is mad. And I suppose, in a way, they are true. But I do not wish to be remembered as a madman. I wish to be remembered as a father, as a man who loved you more than the stars above.

Five years ago, I stood at the edge of the cliff, watching the sun set over the horizon, and I thought of you. You were only sixteen then, and I had hoped to see you again before the winter came. But you left. Not in anger, I believe—though I have no way of knowing. You said you needed to find your own path, your own life. You said you could not stay here, where the silence is louder

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with a hand that trembles not only from the chill of the wind but from the weight of years unspoken. Five years, Eliza. Five long, silent years. I have not seen you, nor heard your voice, nor felt the warmth of your presence in this cold, lonely tower. The sea has been my only companion, and the wind my only friend. But tonight, as the storm gathers strength beyond what I have ever known, I fear the sea may take me before I can say what I must.

You were a child when you left, not by choice, but by necessity. I remember the day you stood at the door, your eyes full of tears, your heart full of anger. You called me a cruel man, a man who had no heart, no soul. I did not deny it. I did not apologize. I could not. I had made my choice, and I had not the strength to change it. But I never stopped loving you, Eliza. I never stopped hoping that one day, you would return.

This lighthouse has stood for over thirty years, weathering every storm that the sea has dared to throw at it. It has endured tempests that would have torn down lesser men. But tonight, I fear that the storm is not only of wind and rain—it is of the soul. I have been alone for so long, and the silence has grown heavier with each passing

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a banshee outside the lighthouse, and the sea roiling as though it would swallow the very rocks upon which I stand. The storm is upon us, and I fear I may not see dawn. But I must write, for if I do not, I fear I shall be left with only silence and the echo of my own voice.

Five years have passed since you left, Eliza. Five long, bitter years. I have not spoken your name aloud in that time, though it lingers in my thoughts like the scent of the sea at dusk. You were once my light, my only child, my greatest joy. And now... now you are but a shadow in my memory, a ghost that haunts the nights when the wind is still and the sea is calm.

I know not what drove you to leave. I know not if you still think of me, or if you have forgotten me entirely. But I cannot let this silence go unanswered. Not when the world is so very uncertain, and I fear I may not live to see the sun rise again.

You were always a curious child, always asking questions. "Why do the waves crash like thunder?" you would ask, wide-eyed and full of wonder. I would tell you that the sea is a living thing, and that it is not cruel, only wild. That it has its own will, and that we

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I write to you now with trembling hands and a heart heavy with the weight of years unspoken. The sea has been restless this evening, and the wind howls like a wounded beast. I have lit the lamp, as I always do, for the safety of those who sail the dark waters, but my own soul feels lost in the storm that is not of the sea.

Five years have passed since the last time we spoke, and though I have not forgotten you, I fear you have forgotten me. I have watched the world change around me—news of the world beyond this rock has come to me in fragments, carried by the occasional sailor or the postman who dares to brave the path. I have read of your life in the city, of your marriage, of the children you have brought into the world. Yet I have not heard your voice, nor seen your face. I have only heard the silence where your words should have been.

I have always been a man of duty, Eliza. My life has been bound to this lighthouse, to the sea, to the light that guides the lost. I have never been one for idle chatter or the comforts of the world beyond these cliffs. But I have felt your absence like a shadow upon my soul. I have wondered if you still remember the nights we spent together as children, when the sea was not a threat, but a companion. When the world felt vast, and

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write to you now with a quill that trembles, not from the wind that howls through the cliffs, but from the weight of silence that has settled between us like the sea before a storm. Five years have passed since you left, and though the days have dragged on, I have not once forgotten your name. Not once.

The lighthouse stands tall, as it always has, though the light within it now flickers with a shadow I cannot name. The sea has always been a cruel mistress, but tonight, it seems to have taken on a new kind of fury. The clouds gather like a shroud, and the wind sings a dirge that I fear is not for the sea alone. I have been watching the horizon for hours, and I do not think I will be able to hold the light through the night. I have not spoken to a soul in this place for so long, and I fear the silence may be louder than the storm.

I have no wish to beg, but I must ask—will you come? Not as a daughter, not as a sister, but as someone who once knew the sound of my voice, who once shared a home with me. I have not seen you in years, and I do not know if I will see you again. But if there is still a part of you that remembers the man I once was, or the father I tried to be, then I beg you to

**April 3rd, 1897**

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I daresay it will find you in the same cold, indifferent place as I have been for these past five years. I have not written to you in such a long time, and yet, as I sit here by the dim glow of the lighthouse lantern, the wind howling like a banshee through the stone walls, I find myself compelled to write. Perhaps it is the storm, or perhaps it is the weight of silence that has settled so heavily upon my heart.

You were always a fine girl, Eliza. I remember the first time I saw you, standing in the doorway of our home, all wrapped in a shawl, your eyes wide with wonder as you gazed at the sea. You were not like the other children who came to visit, who gawked at the lighthouse as if it were a curiosity. You saw something more—something sacred. You understood that the light was not just a beacon, but a promise. A promise that no matter how dark the night, there would always be a way home.

And yet, you left. Left without a word, without a farewell. I remember the day you went—April 1st, 1892. The sea was calm, the sky a pale blue, and I thought for a moment that perhaps this would be the day you

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may not. Five years have passed since I last saw you, and the world has turned upon itself in that time. The sea, which once seemed endless and unchanging, now roars with a fury I have never known. The wind howls like a wounded beast, and the waves claw at the cliffs as if to tear them down. I have kept the light burning all night, as I always have, but I fear the storm may be too much for even the strongest of men—and I am no longer the man I was.

I write this with trembling hands, for I do not know if I will live to see the morning. The lighthouse is old, and the stones beneath my feet are worn by time and salt. I have always believed in the light, in its power to guide, to protect. But tonight, I wonder if it is I who must be guided—by you, by memory, by the love I once thought I had lost.

You were never cruel, Eleanor. I know that now. You left because you could not bear to see me trapped in this life, this endless cycle of duty and solitude. You were right. I was a fool to believe I could find peace in the glare of the lamp. I was a fool to think that the sea could ever fill the hollows in my heart. You were the only one who ever truly saw me,

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart that feels as heavy as the old iron lamp that stands by my window. The sea howls like a wounded beast, and the wind screams as though it knows the truth I've buried deep—of my silence, of my failure, of the love I've let wither like the lighthouse keepers’ flowers in the frost.

Five years, Eleanor. Five long, lonely years since the last time I saw you. I have not spoken to you, nor have I written, for fear that the words might not be enough to mend what has been broken. But tonight, as the storm gathers strength and the waves grow wilder, I feel the weight of my silence pressing down on me. I do not know if I will see dawn.

You were a child when you left, and I was a man who had forgotten how to be a father. I was too busy watching the sea, too wrapped up in the duty that binds me to this stone tower, to see the way your eyes grew distant, the way your laughter faded into something quieter, something like a ghost. I thought that time would heal, that distance would make it easier, that the sea would carry away the ache of your absence. But it only carried away the days, and the nights, and the hope that I might one day find you again.

I have not forgotten the day you left. I remember the way you stood at the

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may not find you at all. Five long years have passed since you left this house, and I have not heard a word from you. The silence has grown heavier with each passing day, like the weight of the sea upon the rocks below. Yet, tonight, as the storm gathers strength beyond the horizon, I find myself compelled to write.

You were always a curious child, always asking questions, always seeking answers beyond what the sea could offer. I remember how you would stand on the cliffs at dawn, watching the waves crash against the rocks, and ask why the light must shine so brightly when the world is already dark. I told you it was to guide the lost and the lonely, to show them the way home. I never thought it would be to guide me.

The winds have grown fierce, and the sea has turned a violent shade of black. The lighthouse stands firm, as it always has, but I can feel it trembling beneath me. I have spent my life tending to this light, and now, as the storm approaches, I wonder if it is the last time I will be able to do so. I have no one to call for help, no one to share the burden of this lonely duty. You were my greatest comfort, Eliza, and now I am left with only the echo of your voice and the memory of your laughter.

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavier than the storm that now gathers on the horizon. Five years have passed since the last letter you sent me, and though the ink has dried on that final missive, the silence between us has grown as vast as the sea. I have not spoken your name aloud in all that time, yet it lingers in my thoughts like the echo of a distant bell.

The wind howls now, a sound that mirrors the tempest within me. I have stood on these cliffs for thirty years, weathering the fury of the sea, but tonight—tonight is different. The sky is black as pitch, the waves rise like mountains, and the wind screams as if it knows the end is near. I have no doubt the sea will claim me before dawn. And yet, I write.

You were never meant to be a child of the sea, Eleanor. You were born for the soft light of the hearth, the warmth of a mother's embrace, the quiet comfort of a home. I should have known. I should have seen it in your eyes, in the way you clung to the stories of the land rather than the tales of the deep. You were not made for the cold, the loneliness, the endless watch. You were made for love, for laughter, for a life that does not end with the tide.

I have spent my years watching the horizon, searching for something—someone

**My Dearest Eliza,**

It is with a heart both heavy and weary that I take up this quill, my hands trembling not from the cold, but from the weight of years unspoken. Five long years have passed since you left this house, and I have not heard a word from you. I have tried, Eliza, I have tried. But the silence between us has grown like a wall, unyielding and cruel.

Tonight, the sky is black as a funeral shroud, and the wind howls like the wails of the damned. The sea is restless, and the lighthouse—my life's work—has stood through many storms. But this one feels different. I have seen the clouds gather in a way that speaks of something more than mere weather. I fear that this night may be the last I shall see the dawn.

I have always been a man of duty, Eliza. I have watched over these rocks, guided the ships, and kept the light burning through every storm. But I have never been able to keep the light of my heart burning for you. I have tried, I have tried to reach out, to explain, to apologize. But you have turned away, and I have not known how to follow.

You were so young when you left, barely more than a child. I remember the day you ran from the house, your tears mingling with the rain, your voice trembling with anger and sorrow. I could not

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I know it may not. I’ve spent these past five years in silence, not out of malice, but because the words would not come. You see, I’ve been a lighthouse keeper for over thirty years, and I’ve learned that the sea does not speak in kind words, nor does it forgive. It takes what it wants, and it takes what it wants when it wants. And tonight, I fear it may take me.

You remember the night you left, don’t you? The storm that raged with such fury that even the lighthouse itself seemed to weep. I stood at the top of the tower, gripping the railing as the wind howled like a banshee, and I could only watch as you ran from the door, your face pale as the moonlight. I tried to call you back, but the wind stole my voice. I tried to chase you, but the stairs were slick with rain, and the lantern’s light was the only thing that remained steady. You never looked back, and I never spoke again.

I know I’ve been a terrible father. I know I’ve let the sea take more than it should. But I’ve tried, Eleanor. I’ve tried to be the man you needed. I’ve kept this lighthouse lit through every storm, every gale, every night of solitude. I’ve watched the stars for years, hoping one

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I sit by the flickering light of the lighthouse, the sea howling like a wounded beast outside, and my heart feels as though it might shatter with the weight of these words. Five years have passed since you left, and yet the echoes of your footsteps still haunt these stone walls. I have not spoken your name in all that time, but I have not forgotten you. Not for a moment.

The storm is upon us, Eliza. The sky is black as a midnight funeral, and the wind howls like the devil himself. I have spent my life watching the sea, guiding ships through the darkest nights, but tonight… tonight I fear I may not be able to guide *myself* through this.

You were always so quick to judge, so quick to run. I remember the day you left—your face pale, your eyes filled with fire. You said I had no soul, that I was a man of stone and salt and silence. You were right, in a way. I have been a man of stone, and the sea has been my only companion. I have not been the father you needed, nor the man you deserved. And for that, I am sorry. I do not know if you can ever forgive me, but I beg you—*please*—know that I have never stopped loving you.

I have seen the world change in these years. The ships grow fewer, the world grows smaller

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence. Five long years have passed since you left this house, and I have not heard a word from you. I do not know if you have forgotten me, or if you have chosen to forget me. But tonight, as the wind howls like a wounded beast and the sea roars with fury, I find myself wondering—what would you say if you knew I was still here?

The storm is coming, Eliza. Not just any storm, but one that has been whispered of in the old sailor’s tales—*the storm that breaks the soul*. The skies have been dark for days, the waves rising higher with each passing hour. I have lit the lamp, as I always do, and the light cuts through the darkness like a blade. It is my duty, my burden, and my only comfort.

I have thought of you often, more than I care to admit. I have watched the sea, hoping to see your face in the mist, or your figure in the glow of the beacon. I have imagined your voice, soft and steady, telling me that you forgive me. That you understand. That you still love me.

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

It has been five long years since I last saw you, and five years since I last spoke to you with any hope of your hearing me. The world has changed, and so has I, though I fear I have not changed for the better. I write this letter now, not with the hope of mending what has been broken between us, but with the knowledge that I may not live to see the dawn of tomorrow. A terrible storm is upon us, and the sea, as always, is a fickle and cruel mistress.

You must know, my dear, that I have not forgotten you. Not in the least. I have kept your portrait upon the wall of this lighthouse, a small frame, but a constant reminder of the daughter I once had, and the daughter I no longer know. You were a bright light in my life, and I have spent these years in darkness, yearning for the day when I might once again be the father you deserve.

I have often wondered what path you have taken. Whether you have found peace, or whether you still carry the weight of our last parting upon your shoulders. I know I was harsh, and I know I was wrong to let pride and anger cloud my judgment. I was a fool to think that a man’s pride could ever outweigh the love of a child. I was wrong, and I have spent these years in regret, in silence, in the bitter cold of my own

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I sit by the flickering light of the lighthouse, the wind howling like a vengeful spirit against the stone walls. The sea roars with fury, and the sky is dark as a midnight funeral. Tonight, the storm is unlike any I’ve seen in my years here. I fear it may take more than the sea from us.

Five years have passed since you left, Eliza. Five years since you refused to speak to me, and five years since I have dared to write. I have tried, oh how I have tried, to find the words that might mend what has been broken. But the silence between us has grown like a wall of stone, and I have not had the courage to knock.

You were but a child when you ran from this place, from me, from the life we shared. I remember the day you stood at the door, your face pale with fear and anger, and said, “You don’t understand.” And I believed you. I believed that I had failed you, that I had not been a good father, that I had not been the man you deserved.

But I have not forgotten the nights you spent in the lighthouse, listening to the waves, whispering to the sea as if it might carry your voice to the world. I have not forgotten the way you would sit by the light, watching it dance on the water, as if it were a promise that we might still

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may find you in a place I can never hope to reach. Five years have passed since the last time I laid eyes upon you, and yet the weight of that silence still sits heavy upon my chest like a stone. I have tried, Eliza, I have truly tried, to write to you, to find the words that might mend what has been broken between us. But the sea has always been more willing to speak than I have been.

Tonight, the storm is coming. I can feel it in my bones, the way the wind howls like a wounded beast, the way the waves grow restless and the sky darkens with a fury that feels almost human. I have stood on the cliff for hours, watching the horizon, knowing that the sea will not spare me. I have always been its servant, and it has always been my master. But tonight, I fear it may take me before I can finish this letter.

You were never meant to be the daughter of a man who lives alone with the sea. You were meant to have a mother’s voice, a father’s embrace, a home filled with laughter and light. I gave you the world, and then I gave you nothing. I let pride and duty blind me, and in doing so, I lost you. I have no excuse, no justification, only the bitter truth that I was a fool.

I have tried to forgive you, but the bitterness of your departure lingers like the salt in my wounds. You were my light, Eleanor. And now, I find myself adrift in darkness once more.

I have not spoken of your father. Not since the day you chose to leave. But I have thought of him often. The way he would sit by the window, staring at the sea, as if searching for something he had lost. I never understood the weight of his sorrow until I saw the way you carried it in your eyes. You were not the first to leave, and you will not be the last. But I cannot bear to think of you as a ghost, haunting the edges of my life.

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a wounded beast against the lighthouse walls. The sea has never been so restless, and the sky is a bruised canvas of black and green. I fear the night will not end kindly. But I must write—because I cannot bear the thought of you never knowing the truth, even if it is too late.

Five years have passed since you left, and I have not spoken to you in that time. I have not spoken to *anyone* in that time. The silence has grown heavier than the stone walls of this lighthouse, and I have come to believe that silence is the only thing that keeps me from drowning in the guilt that swells in my chest like a storm.

You were fifteen when you ran, Eleanor. You were angry. You were hurt. I know that now. But I also know that I was not the father you needed. I was a man too wrapped in duty, too blind to see the fire in your eyes when you looked at me. I thought I was protecting you, but I was only pushing you away. I thought I was strong enough to keep the light burning, but I was not strong enough to keep the love in my heart.

My Dearest Clara,  

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence. It has been five long years since we last spoke, and I fear the storm that brews on the horizon may not be the only thing to claim me. But if I should fall, I wish for you to know that I have never stopped thinking of you, even when the sea and the silence between us seemed to swallow my voice whole.  

You were never meant to be a stranger, my dear. I know the world has its ways of twisting love into something unrecognizable, and I know that you, with your sharp tongue and your stubborn pride, have been wounded by the things I said and the things I failed to say. But I must tell you now, in this moment when the wind howls like a beast and the waves crash against the cliffs as though they wish to tear us all apart, that I have always loved you. I have always wanted you to be happy, even if I could not be the father you needed.  

I have spent these years watching the sea, Clara, and I have come to understand its language. It speaks in whispers and roars, in the crash of waves and the sigh of the tide. **To My Dearest Eliza,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may find you in a place of heartache, as it finds me. It has been five long, bitter years since we last spoke, and though the days have dragged on like the fog that clings to these cliffs, I have not ceased to think of you, my dear Eliza.

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I sit by the flickering light of the lighthouse, the wind howling like a wounded beast against the stone walls, and I find myself writing to you. **April 12th, 1893**  
**The Lighthouse, St. Agnes Head**  
**Isle of Wight, England**

My Dearest Eleanor,

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence. It has been five long, bitter years since you left this house, and though I have kept the lighthouse burning for all these months, I have not kept the light of your presence. I fear now that the storm which gathers on the horizon may be more than a threat to the coast — it may be the final end of me.

You remember the day you left, do you not? That cold, grey morning when you stood at the door with your father’s coat still on your back, and your eyes held the fire of a woman who had already decided to walk away. I told you I would not let you go, but I was wrong. I was too proud, too stubborn, too afraid to admit that I had failed you. And so you left, and I let you.

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I sit here by the light, the sea howling like a wounded beast, and the wind rattling the shutters as though it would tear this old house apart. I’ve lit the lamp, as I always do, though I fear it may be the last time I can. The storm is coming—something I’ve not seen in these parts in years, and it is said to be unlike any other. The tide is rising, the waves are black as ink, and the sky is split by lightning like the very hand of God Himself. I pray you are safe, wherever you are.

Five years have passed since you left. Five years of silence, of letters unanswered, of your name on my lips like a curse. I have not seen you since that last night, when you stood at the door, your eyes like cold steel, and told me you were leaving. You said I had no right to ask you to stay, that I had become a relic, a man who had forgotten how to love. You were right, Eliza. I have forgotten. I have forgotten how to love, how to forgive, how to be anything but a man who keeps the light burning for those who don’t know it’s there.

I have always been the keeper of this light. I have always been the one to watch the sea, to listen to its whispers, to whisper back. But you—*you* were the light I never knew
